basmala: In the Name of God, the Infinitely Merciful, the Most Merciful.
intro: al-Ḥāqqa — “the Inevitable” — takes its name, says Qushayrī, from the fact that “it makes every person liable for his deed, good or evil”: what must come will come, what is due will be rendered. The sūra recalls the annihilated peoples — Thamūd, ʿĀd, Pharaoh —, reads in Noah's ark an image of the salvation of the intimates “borne in the ship of well-being while the cosmos struggles in the waves of dispersion,” then describes the Day: the trumpet sounded, the earth and mountains pulverized, the heaven rent, the Throne borne by eight. Then comes the parting of the two books: the one received in the right hand — “here, read my book!” — and the one of the left hand. Qushayrī grafts onto this a moving pearl about the forsaken: there are today men kept at a distance, whose sighs rise and whose anguish redoubles; they think themselves rejected — and they are, in truth, the mercied of God: He has let the veil down upon them, and made them small in their own eyes — they who are the noblest of the tale. The sūra ends by affirming that this Quran is revelation from the Lord of the worlds, “the Truth of certainty,” and the command to glorify the Name of the immense Lord.
themes: the parting of the two books

versets.trad: The Inevitable!
versets.commentaire: al-ḥāqqa (الحَاقَّة): the Inevitable, that which comes to pass without fail. Qushayrī: “it is a name of the Resurrection, for it makes every person liable for his deed, good or evil.” The root ḥaqq speaks of the true, the due, what imposes itself. The sūra opens on this single word, hurled like a blow: this Day is the one on which everything becomes exigible, on which each act claims its account. The Inevitable is no distant threat but the underlying truth of all life: what one has done will come back to oneself.

mots.def: ḥāqqa: that which necessarily comes to pass, that makes due.
mots.gram: active participle fem., proper name of the Day

versets.trad: What is the Inevitable?
versets.commentaire: mā l-ḥāqqa (ما الحَاقَّة): what is the Inevitable? The question takes up the word to deepen its import. It is no request for information but a device of amplification: to repeat the name, to suspend it in a question, is to magnify its gravity. Even before describing the Day, the Quran lets its immensity be sensed by this hammering. The repetition of the word speaks of the scale of what no speech can contain.

versets.trad: And what will make you know what the Inevitable is?
versets.commentaire: wa-mā adrāka (وَما أَدْراكَ): what will make you know? Qushayrī: “an interrogation that serves to magnify its affair, to enlarge its importance.” As for Saqar (s. 74, v. 27) and the Day of Decision (s. 77, v. 14), the formula does not answer but deepens the mystery. The Inevitable surpasses all the mind can conceive; even to the Prophet, its full reality is veiled. The threefold call — the Inevitable, what is it, what will make you know it — installs a solemn gravity before the account of the annihilated peoples.

versets.trad: Thamūd and ʿĀd denied the Striking Calamity.
versets.commentaire: al-qāriʿa (القارِعَة): the great striking, that which strikes — another name of the Day. Qushayrī: “the sūra mentions those who denied their messengers, persisted in their denial, did not accept the counsel of their prophets; He annihilated them, and avenged His prophets. The profit of their mention is the lesson (iʿtibār) one draws from it, the guarding against what they did, so as not to undergo what they underwent.” Thamūd and ʿĀd, mighty peoples, denied the striking Day — and that Day reached them otherwise, by their very destruction. To deny the Inevitable does not avert it; it hastens its coming.

mots.def: kadhdhaba: to treat as a lie.
qāriʿa: that which strikes, the cataclysm.

versets.trad: As for Thamūd, they were destroyed by the overwhelming blast.
versets.commentaire: bi-l-ṭāghiya (بِالطّاغِيَة): by the cataclysm exceeding all measure (the thunderbolt-cry). The word shares the root of ṭughyān, excess: Thamūd, who had overstepped the bounds, were seized by an overwhelming force. The sanction espouses the fault: to the excess of pride answers the excess of chastisement. What struck them bore, in its very name, the echo of their transgression.

mots.racine: ه ل ك / ط غ ي
mots.sens: they were destroyed by the overwhelming blast
mots.def: ahlaka: to destroy; ṭāghiya: that which exceeds all measure.

versets.ar: وَأَمَّا عَادٌ فَأُهْلِكُوا بِرِيحٍ صَرْصَرٍ عَاتِيَةٍ
versets.tr: wa-ammā ʿādun fa-uhlikū bi-rīḥin ṣarṣarin ʿātiyatin
versets.trad: As for ʿĀd, they were destroyed by a howling, raging wind
versets.commentaire: rīḥ ṣarṣar ʿātiya (ريح صَرْصَر عاتِيَة): an icy, howling, raging wind. Qushayrī gives here a striking inner reading: just as ʿĀd were destroyed by the wind, “some of the intimates, if they divulge a secret or neglect an adab, are chastised by the winds of veiling (riyāḥ al-ḥijba): no trace remains in their heart of reverence for religion or of their past states.” The wind that ravages without has its counterpart within: there are spiritual gusts that strip the heart of its lights, when it has failed in the guarding of the secret.

mots.racine: ه ل ك / ص ر ص ر / ع ت و
mots.sens: destroyed by a howling, raging wind
mots.def: ṣarṣar: icy, loud wind; ʿātiya: unleashed.

versets.ar: سَخَّرَهَا عَلَيْهِمْ سَبْعَ لَيَالٍ وَثَمَانِيَةَ أَيَّامٍ حُسُومًا فَتَرَى الْقَوْمَ فِيهَا صَرْعَىٰ كَأَنَّهُمْ أَعْجَازُ نَخْلٍ خَاوِيَةٍ
versets.tr: sakhkharahā ʿalayhim sabʿa layālin wa-thamāniyata ayyāmin ḥusūman fa-tarā l-qawma fīhā ṣarʿā kaʾannahum aʿjāzu nakhlin khāwiyatin
versets.trad: which God let loose against them for seven nights and eight days without ceasing. Then you would have seen those people laid low, like hollow trunks of palm trees.
versets.commentaire: kaʾannahum aʿjāzu nakhlin khāwiya (كَأَنَّهُمْ أَعْجازُ نَخْلٍ خاوِيَة): like hollow palm-trunks, felled. A striking image: this once-vigorous people, proud giants, lie on the ground like emptied stumps. Ḥusūman: without respite, a duration that “cuts” clean. The wind blew seven nights and eight days, until nothing was left. The physical greatness of ʿĀd weighed nothing: a breath of God laid them low. The image of the hollow palm speaks of the emptiness of every force that thinks itself autonomous: majestic without, void within, it collapses at the first wind of the Real.

mots.sens: He let it loose against them
seven nights and eight days without ceasing

mots.racine: ع ج ز / ن خ ل / خ و ي
mots.def: aʿjāz nakhl: palm stumps; khāwiya: hollow.

versets.trad: Do you see any of them remaining?
versets.commentaire: hal tarā lahum min bāqiya (هَلْ تَرى لَهُمْ مِنْ باقِيَة): do you see a single one of them remaining? A question whose answer is the void. Of that immense people, nothing remains. The power that seemed eternal has vanished without a trace. The question addresses the present: what became of their force, their pride? Nothing. Such is the lesson of ruins: what thinks itself indestructible passes, and only the One who does not pass remains. Permanence belongs to God alone.

versets.ar: وَجَاءَ فِرْعَوْنُ وَمَن قَبْلَهُ وَالْمُؤْتَفِكَاتُ بِالْخَاطِئَةِ
versets.tr: wa-jāʾa firʿawnu wa-man qablahu wa-l-muʾtafikātu bi-l-khāṭiʾati
versets.trad: Pharaoh, those before him, and the overturned cities committed sin:
versets.commentaire: wa-l-muʾtafikāt bi-l-khāṭiʾa (وَالمُؤْتَفِكاتُ بِالخاطِئَة): the overturned cities, by their sin. To the peoples of Thamūd and ʿĀd are added Pharaoh and the cities of Lot's people, “overturned” from top to bottom. Qushayrī had noted that Pharaoh “was chastised by drowning; likewise, one who had a free time, occupied with the obedience of his Lord, but who does not give thanks for the blessing, neglects his adab and misjudges the value of the grace received, God returns him to the causes of dispersion, then drowns him in the seas of preoccupation.” Every outward destruction carries an inner teaching: to turn away from God after having known Him drowns the heart.

mots.racine: ج ي أ / ق ب ل
mots.sens: Pharaoh came, and those before him

versets.ar: فَعَصَوْا رَسُولَ رَبِّهِمْ فَأَخَذَهُمْ أَخْذَةً رَّابِيَةً
versets.tr: fa-ʿaṣaw rasūla rabbihim fa-akhadhahum akhdhatan rābiyatan
versets.trad: they disobeyed the messenger of their Lord, and He seized them with an overwhelming grip.
versets.commentaire: akhdhatan rābiya (أَخْذَةً رابِيَة): a crushing seizure, that overflows. All these peoples have a common fault: fa-ʿaṣaw rasūla rabbihim — they disobeyed the messenger of their Lord. The root of the disaster is always there: to refuse the messenger sent. The seizure that follows is “rābiya,” that grows and overflows — to the measure of the transgression. The law is constant across history: to each people its messenger, to each refusal its consequence. To disobey the bearer of the truth is to call down upon oneself the seizure of the One who sends him.

mots.racine: ع ص ي / ر س ل
mots.sens: they disobeyed the messenger of their Lord

mots.racine: أ خ ذ / ر ب و
mots.sens: He seized them with an overwhelming grip

versets.ar: إِنَّا لَمَّا طَغَى الْمَاءُ حَمَلْنَاكُمْ فِي الْجَارِيَةِ
versets.tr: innā lammā ṭaghā l-māʾu ḥamalnākum fī l-jāriyati
versets.trad: But We, when the water overflowed, bore you upon the vessel,
versets.commentaire: ḥamalnākum fī l-jāriya (حَمَلْناكُمْ في الجارِيَة): We bore you in the vessel. To the flood that engulfs the sinners answers the ark that saves. Qushayrī gives a splendid inner reading: “such is His grace upon the elite of His saints, when He sets them safe in the ship of well-being (safīnat al-ʿāfiya), while the cosmos struggles in the waves of the seas of preoccupation, in their various forms: they remain in safety, with neither dispute nor account to settle with anyone, expecting nothing from anyone — safe from people, and people safe from them.” Noah's ark is the image of the heart God keeps amid the storms of the world: borne, sheltered, at peace.

mots.racine: ط غ ي / م و ه
ح م ل / ج ر ي
mots.sens: We bore you in the vessel
mots.def: ḥamala: to bear; jāriya: a vessel that sails.

versets.ar: لِنَجْعَلَهَا لَكُمْ تَذْكِرَةً وَتَعِيَهَا أُذُنٌ وَاعِيَةٌ
versets.tr: li-najʿalahā lakum tadhkiratan wa-taʿiyahā udhunun wāʿiyatun
versets.trad: to make it a reminder for you, and that a retaining ear might retain it.
versets.commentaire: taʿiyahā udhunun wāʿiya (تَعِيَها أُذُنٌ واعِيَة): that a retaining ear might recollect it. The ark is made a “reminder” (tadhkira): a sign to ponder. But the sign reaches only the ear that retains (wāʿiya) — the one that not only hears but keeps, holds, ponders. As in al-Mulk (s. 67, v. 10), true listening is a spiritual sense: it is not enough that the sound enter, the heart must conserve it. The same reminder passes over all, but only the living ear makes it its own. All depends on the quality of listening.

mots.racine: ج ع ل / ذ ك ر
mots.sens: to make it a reminder for you

mots.racine: و ع ي / أ ذ ن
mots.sens: that a retaining ear might retain it
mots.def: waʿā: to retain, recollect; wāʿiya: that conserves.

versets.ar: فَإِذَا نُفِخَ فِي الصُّورِ نَفْخَةٌ وَاحِدَةٌ
versets.tr: fa-idhā nufikha fī l-ṣūri nafkhatun wāḥidatun
versets.trad: When the trumpet is sounded a single blast,
versets.commentaire: nafkhatun wāḥida (نَفْخَةٌ واحِدَة): a single blast. All that men think solid collapses at one single blast in the trumpet. As in al-Nāziʿāt (s. 79), no effort is needed: a single cry, and the order of the world comes undone. The sūra passes from the account of past peoples to the horizon of the Day: what struck them one by one will strike the whole universe at once. The singleness of the blast speaks of the absolute ease with which God undoes what He has made.

mots.racine: ن ف خ / ص و ر
ن ف خ / و ح د

versets.ar: وَحُمِلَتِ الْأَرْضُ وَالْجِبَالُ فَدُكَّتَا دَكَّةً وَاحِدَةً
versets.tr: wa-ḥumilati l-arḍu wa-l-jibālu fa-dukkatā dakkatan wāḥidatan
versets.trad: when the earth and the mountains are lifted and crushed in a single blow,
versets.commentaire: fa-dukkatā dakkatan wāḥida (فَدُكَّتا دَكَّةً واحِدَة): crushed in a single grinding. The earth and the mountains — the very symbols of stability — are lifted then ground at once. All that man leans upon gives way. Like the hollow palm of ʿĀd (v. 7), the solidity of the world proves illusory on the Day. A single blow suffices to reduce to dust what seemed eternal. Only the reality founded in God remains firm; all the rest is ground away.

mots.racine: ح م ل / أ ر ض / ج ب ل
mots.sens: the earth and mountains are lifted

versets.trad: that Day the Event will come to pass;
versets.commentaire: waqaʿati l-wāqiʿa (وَقَعَتِ الواقِعَة): the Event came to pass. Al-Wāqiʿa — that which falls, comes to pass without fail — is yet another name of the Day (the title of sūra 56). After the blast and the grinding, the Event occurs: what was announced becomes present. All the names of the Day — the Inevitable, the Striking, the Event — speak of the same certainty under different aspects. What was to fall falls: the promise is realized, and none can any longer push it away.

mots.def: waqaʿa: to fall, occur; wāqiʿa: the Event.

versets.trad: the heaven will be split, and that Day it will be frail.
versets.commentaire: fa-hiya yawmaʾidhin wāhiya (فَهِيَ يَوْمَئِذٍ واهِيَة): and that Day it will be frail, distended. The heaven, the vault that seemed the most solid and the highest, splits and becomes thin as worn cloth. What seemed unshakeable proves frail. On the Day, the order of grandeurs is inverted: the highest gives way. The sudden frailty of the heaven says that no created structure has solidity of its own; all holds only by the hand that maintains it (cf. the birds, s. 67, v. 19), and that hand, on this Day, releases what it held.

mots.racine: ش ق ق / س م و

mots.sens: that day it will be frail

versets.ar: وَالْمَلَكُ عَلَىٰ أَرْجَائِهَا وَيَحْمِلُ عَرْشَ رَبِّكَ فَوْقَهُمْ يَوْمَئِذٍ ثَمَانِيَةٌ
versets.tr: wa-l-malaku ʿalā arjāʾihā wa-yaḥmilu ʿarsha rabbika fawqahum yawmaʾidhin thamāniyatun
versets.trad: The angels will stand at its edges, and that Day eight will bear above them the Throne of your Lord.
versets.commentaire: yaḥmilu ʿarsha rabbika fawqahum yawmaʾidhin thamāniya (يَحْمِلُ عَرْشَ رَبِّكَ فَوْقَهُمْ يَوْمَئِذٍ ثَمانِيَة): eight will bear the Throne of your Lord. While the heaven is rent, the angels ranged at the edges of what remains, and eight of them bear the Throne. An image of sovereign majesty amid the universal collapse: all comes undone, but the Throne — symbol of the Reign — remains, borne, raised. When all structures give way, God's lordship manifests in its full grandeur. The number eight remains mysterious; what matters is that the Throne does not fall.

mots.racine: م ل ك / ر ج و
ح م ل / ع ر ش
mots.sens: they bear the Throne of your Lord above them

versets.ar: يَوْمَئِذٍ تُعْرَضُونَ لَا تَخْفَىٰ مِنكُمْ خَافِيَةٌ
versets.tr: yawmaʾidhin tuʿraḍūna lā takhfā minkum khāfiyatun
versets.trad: That Day you will be brought forth, and no secret of yours will stay hidden.
versets.commentaire: lā takhfā minkum khāfiya (لا تَخْفى مِنْكُمْ خافِيَة): no secret of yours will remain hidden. The Day is one of total unveiling: one is brought forth (tuʿraḍūn), laid bare, so that nothing of the inmost self can remain veiled. What one hid from men, and sometimes from oneself, appears in full light. But the One before whom all is unveiled already knew everything (cf. s. 67, v. 13): the Day teaches Him nothing; it only reveals to each what he was. To live in the awareness that nothing is hidden is to begin even now to stand in the truth.

mots.sens: that day you will be brought forth
no secret of yours stays hidden

versets.ar: فَأَمَّا مَنْ أُوتِيَ كِتَابَهُ بِيَمِينِهِ فَيَقُولُ هَاؤُمُ اقْرَءُوا كِتَابِيَهْ
versets.tr: fa-ammā man ūtiya kitābahu bi-yamīnihi fa-yaqūlu hāʾumu qraʾū kitābiyah
versets.trad: As for the one given his book in his right hand, he will say: “Here, read my book!
themes: the book of the right hand
versets.commentaire: hāʾumu qraʾū kitābiyah (هاؤُمُ اقْرَؤوا كِتابِيَهْ): here, read my book! A cry of joy from the one who receives his record in his right hand: he offers it proudly to be read, having nothing to hide. Qushayrī: “the happiness of God's grace is acquired by him, and he sets about praise.” The total unveiling (v. 18), which terrifies the guilty, gladdens the righteous: for him, to have nothing hidden is a liberation, not a shame. He invites others to read his book — a gesture of happy transparency. The one who has lived under the Gaze has nothing to dread from the unveiling.

mots.racine: أ ت ي / ك ت ب / ي م ن
mots.sens: the one given his book in his right hand
mots.def: ūtiya: to be given; yamīn: right hand.

mots.racine: ق و ل / ق ر أ
mots.sens: he says: here, read my book
mots.def: hāʾum: here, take; qaraʾa: to read.

versets.trad: I was sure that I would meet my reckoning!”,
versets.commentaire: ẓanantu annī mulāqin ḥisābiyah (ظَنَنْتُ أَنّي مُلاقٍ حِسابِيَهْ): I was sure I would meet my account. The secret of his joy: he lived awaiting this Day. Ẓanantu here is not doubt but lived certainty: he held himself, all his life, in the awareness that he would render account. It is the exact opposite of the deniers who “expected no account” (s. 78, v. 27). To have lived awaiting the account is to have prepared for it; and whoever has prepared for it welcomes it with joy. The certainty of the return, kept in life, becomes serenity on the Day.

mots.racine: ل ق ي / ح س ب
mots.sens: that I would meet my account

versets.trad: and he will live content
versets.commentaire: fī ʿīshatin rāḍiya (في عيشَةٍ راضِيَة): in a pleasing life. Qushayrī finely distinguishes: the blessed will tomorrow be in a pleasing life because “their needs are fulfilled, their desires satisfied”; but the realized are already today in a pleasing life, for “they have stilled their desires: God has removed their needs from their heart, they have no will, no lack touches them — they are in the rest of contentment (riḍā).” And he concludes: “the life of the one is in the gift, that of the other in contentment.” The highest is not to obtain what one desires, but to have nothing left to desire but God.

mots.racine: ع ي ش / ر ض و
mots.sens: he is in a pleasing life

versets.trad: in a lofty Garden,
versets.commentaire: fī jannatin ʿāliya (في جَنَّةٍ عالِيَة): in a lofty Garden. The Garden is high: elevation of place and of rank. After the contented life of the heart (v. 21), the dwelling that befits it. The elevation of the Garden answers the elevation of aspiration: whoever has borne his heart toward the heights finds his dwelling there. The Garden is not only a place of pleasure but a place of height — near the Throne, far from the baseness of the world. One dwells to the measure of what one has reached for.

mots.racine: ج ن ن / ع ل و

versets.trad: its fruits within reach.
versets.commentaire: quṭūfuhā dāniya (قُطوفُها دانِيَة): its fruits are close at hand. In this lofty Garden, the fruits bend toward the hand. As in al-Insān (s. 76, v. 14), the fruit becomes docile. A luminous paradox: the Garden is high (v. 22), and yet its fruits are within reach. Elevation does not distance the gift; it brings it near. Where here below one must toil to reach, there desire is anticipated. The elevated heart need no longer strain toward what it loves: all comes to it.

mots.racine: ق ط ف / د ن و
mots.def: quṭūf: clusters to pick; dāniya: near.

versets.ar: كُلُوا وَاشْرَبُوا هَنِيئًا بِمَا أَسْلَفْتُمْ فِي الْأَيَّامِ الْخَالِيَةِ
versets.tr: kulū wa-shrabū hanīʾan bimā aslaftum fī l-ayyāmi l-khāliyati
versets.trad: “Eat and drink in peace, for what you sent ahead in the days gone by.”
versets.commentaire: bimā aslaftum fī l-ayyāmi l-khāliya (بِما أَسْلَفْتُمْ في الأَيّامِ الخالِيَة): for what you sent ahead in the days gone by. Qushayrī raises the sense: “they will be told: listen and contemplate… listen to Us, gaze upon Us, find the intimacy of Our nearness, contemplate Our beauty and Our majesty; you are by Us and for Us.” The feast of the Garden is but the sensible veil of a higher intimacy. Aslaftum — you sent ahead: what one sowed in “the days gone by” of life is reaped here. The days spent working for God are not lost: they are the advance one finds again, multiplied, at the end.

mots.racine: أ ك ل / ش ر ب / ه ن أ

mots.def: aslafa: to send ahead, do in advance.

versets.ar: وَأَمَّا مَنْ أُوتِيَ كِتَابَهُ بِشِمَالِهِ فَيَقُولُ يَا لَيْتَنِي لَمْ أُوتَ كِتَابِيَهْ
versets.tr: wa-ammā man ūtiya kitābahu bi-shimālihi fa-yaqūlu yā laytanī lam ūta kitābiyah
versets.trad: But as for the one given his book in his left hand, he will say: “Woe to me!... Would that I had not been given my book
themes: the book of the left hand
versets.commentaire: yā laytanī lam ūta kitābiyah (يا لَيْتَني لَمْ أوتَ كِتابِيَهْ): would that I had not been given my book! The exact reverse of the joy of verse 19: the one who receives his record in his left hand would wish never to have received it. The unveiling (v. 18) that gladdens the righteous overwhelms the guilty. But Qushayrī opens here, for the following verses, an unexpected and moving reading about the “forsaken” (mahjūrīn): not all who think themselves rejected are so in truth. Regret may be, for certain loving hearts, the sign of a grace hidden beneath the veil of trial.

mots.racine: أ ت ي / ك ت ب / ش م ل
mots.sens: the one given his book in his left hand

mots.racine: أ ت ي / ك ت ب
mots.sens: would that I had not been given my book

versets.trad: and never known my reckoning!
versets.commentaire: wa-lam adri mā ḥisābiyah (وَلَمْ أَدْرِ ما حِسابِيَهْ): and never known my account! The guilty would wish to be ignorant of his own reckoning, to flee the knowledge that condemns him. It is the reverse of the righteous who “was sure he would meet his account” (v. 20) and awaited it. The one who did not live in awareness of the return would now escape the truth — but it imposes itself. To flee one's account on the Day is as vain as to have denied it in life. One cannot indefinitely hide from oneself what one is.

versets.trad: Would that it had been the end of all!
versets.commentaire: yā laytahā kānati l-qāḍiya (يا لَيْتَها كانَتِ القاضِيَة): would that death had been final! He wishes his first death had been a total annihilation, with no resurrection. It is the height of despair: to prefer nothingness to an existence become unbearable. But it is here that Qushayrī opens his pearl about the forsaken (v. 25-27): “there are today men kept at a distance, whose sighs rise, whose anguish redoubles night and day; their night is woe, their day distance; their springs are troubled, the dwellings of their intimacy ruined; none pities their weeping, none hears their groaning. They think themselves rejected — and they are, in truth, the mercied of God: He has let the veil down upon them (asbala ʿalayhim al-sitr), and made them small in their own eyes — they who are the noblest of the tale.” And he cites this verse: “Do not deny my denial of your love: that very denial is a veil let down upon you.” Sometimes the appearance of rejection covers the tenderest of graces.

mots.sens: would that death had been final
mots.def: qāḍiya: that which cuts off, ends definitively.

versets.trad: My wealth has availed me nothing
versets.commentaire: mā aghnā ʿannī māliyah (ما أَغْنى عَنّي مالِيَهْ): my wealth has availed me nothing. On the Day, the amassed wealth does not save. What the guilty had founded his power upon proves worthless. The possessions that reassured, that gave prestige and security, cannot ransom a single ounce of the chastisement. It is the confession of the vanity of all that was not turned toward God. Wealth was an illusory support: at the moment one most needs it, it weighs nothing. Only what one sent ahead matters (v. 24).

mots.def: aghnā: to be of use, dispense.

versets.trad: and my power has deserted me!”
versets.commentaire: halaka ʿannī sulṭāniyah (هَلَكَ عَنّي سُلْطانِيَهْ): my power has vanished. After wealth (v. 28), authority: it too collapses. The guilty may have commanded, dominated, imposed his law; on the Day, all that has dissipated. The sulṭān — power, argument, force — that made him feared is of no avail. The two pillars of worldly power, having and power, fall together. What raised man above others raises him in nothing before God. The only authority that remains is that of the King of the Kingdom (s. 67, v. 1).

versets.trad: “Seize him and shackle him!
versets.commentaire: khudhūhu fa-ghullūhu (خُذوهُ فَغُلّوهُ): seize him, fetter him. The sentence falls, brief and implacable. The fetter (ghull) put on him is the image of the shackle his own attachments forged: whoever bound himself to his wealth and power (v. 28-29) finds himself bound on the Day. The pride that would not bow (cf. s. 68, v. 42) is now bowed by force. The outer fetter is but the manifestation of the inner chains the soul had given itself. One wears outwardly the shackles one has woven within.

versets.trad: then cast him into the Blaze,
versets.commentaire: thumma l-jaḥīma ṣallūhu (ثُمَّ الجَحيمَ صَلّوهُ): then burn him in the Blaze. The sentence is carried out: after the fetter (v. 30), the fire. The guilty, stripped of his supports (v. 28-29) and bound, is delivered to the blaze. The progression is inexorable, but it is only the logical sequel of what precedes: whoever fed within himself the fire of passions without turning it toward God reaps its flame. The Blaze is no arbitrary addition; it is the term of what the heart has nursed. One enters the fire one has oneself fed.

versets.ar: ثُمَّ فِي سِلْسِلَةٍ ذَرْعُهَا سَبْعُونَ ذِرَاعًا فَاسْلُكُوهُ
versets.tr: thumma fī silsilatin dharʿuhā sabʿūna dhirāʿan fa-slukūhu
versets.trad: then bind him in a chain seventy cubits long.”
versets.commentaire: silsilatin dharʿuhā sabʿūna dhirāʿan (سِلْسِلَةٍ ذَرْعُها سَبْعونَ ذِراعًا): a chain seventy cubits long. The image of total binding: the guilty is enclosed in a measureless chain. The number, hyperbolic, speaks of the scale of the shackle. Spiritually, the length of the chain measures the thickness of the accumulated attachments: the more one has chained oneself to the world, the longer the chain. The one who multiplied bonds — wealth, power, passions — finds himself wound in their sum. The freedom refused to God reverses into a servitude without measure.

versets.ar: إِنَّهُ كَانَ لَا يُؤْمِنُ بِاللَّهِ الْعَظِيمِ
versets.tr: innahu kāna lā yuʾminu bi-llāhi l-ʿaẓīmi
versets.trad: He did not believe in God the Magnificent,
versets.commentaire: kāna lā yuʾminu bi-llāhi l-ʿaẓīm (كانَ لا يُؤْمِنُ بِالله العَظيم): he did not believe in God the Immense. Here at last is the cause of it all: the absence of faith. Before the faults, before the refusal to feed the poor (v. 34), there was this first lack — not to believe in God, the Magnificent. The root of the disaster is not this or that sin but the heart closed to the grandeur of God. All the rest — avarice, pride, attachment to possessions — flows from this initial void. Whoever does not acknowledge the grandeur of God makes himself his own god, and all follows.

mots.gram: inna + verb of state + negation + verb

versets.trad: and he did not urge the feeding of the poor.
versets.commentaire: wa-lā yaḥuḍḍu ʿalā ṭaʿāmi l-miskīn (وَلا يَحُضُّ عَلى طَعامِ المِسْكين): he did not urge the feeding of the poor. The second cause, twin of the first: after the rupture with God (v. 33), the rupture with the neighbour. And the verse goes further than sūra al-Māʿūn: it reproaches not only the failure to feed the poor, but the failure even to urge others to do so. The indifference is total: neither gesture nor word in favour of the needy. Whoever has closed his heart to God closes it to the poor. The two bonds — toward the high and toward the low — break together.

mots.racine: ط ع م / س ك ن

versets.trad: Today he has here no devoted friend,
versets.commentaire: laysa lahu l-yawma hāhunā ḥamīm (لَيْسَ لَهُ اليَوْمَ هاهُنا حَميم): he has today here no intimate friend. A consequence of his indifference to the poor (v. 34): none cares for him on the Day. The one who held out his hand to no one finds no hand held out. The ḥamīm — the warm friend, the devoted intimate — is lacking. The solitude of the guilty on the Day is the exact reflection of the solitude in which he left others: he closed his heart, his heart is left alone. The bonds one did not tie in life are missing at the moment one would need them.

versets.trad: nor any food but a foul discharge
versets.commentaire: illā min ghislīn (إلّا مِنْ غِسْلين): except ghislīn (foul discharge, festering pus). A striking reversal: he who did not urge the feeding of the poor (v. 34) now has, for all food, but a foul liquid. The deprivation of food he let others suffer turns back against him. The gift of food refused becomes foul food received. The justice of the Day espouses the fault exactly: whoever did not give food finds himself with nothing to eat but horror. One reaps the opposite of what one refused to sow.

versets.trad: which none but the sinners eat.
versets.commentaire: lā yaʾkuluhu illā l-khāṭiʾūn (لا يَأْكُلُهُ إلّا الخاطِئون): none eats it but the sinners. This foul food is reserved for those whose fault (khaṭīʾa) is deliberate. The word khāṭiʾ denotes the one who does evil knowingly, by choice. The food of the Fire does not fall by chance: it is the lot of those who did evil in full awareness. The consequence is fitted to the responsibility: one receives the ghislīn only if one has, in full consciousness, closed one's heart to God and to the poor. The lucid fault calls forth the exact requital.

versets.trad: So I swear by what you see
versets.commentaire: fa-lā uqsimu bimā tubṣirūn (فَلا أُقْسِمُ بِما تُبْصِرون): I swear by what you see. Qushayrī: “the lā here is connective; the meaning: I swear — as if He said: I swear by all things, for there is no third term between the visible and the invisible.” God swears by the visible world. After the account of the two destinies, the oath introduces the decisive affirmation about the Quran (v. 40). To swear by the visible and the invisible is to swear by the totality of existence: the amplest oath for the highest truth.

versets.trad: and by what you do not see:
versets.commentaire: wa-mā lā tubṣirūn (وَما لا تُبْصِرون): and by what you do not see. The oath now embraces the invisible. Qushayrī: “by all things, for there is no third term to what is seen and what is not seen.” The visible and the invisible exhaust the real: to swear by both is to swear by all that is. The total oath prepares the total truth about to be affirmed. And it recalls in passing that the invisible is as real as the visible: what the eye does not perceive is no less part of the real by which God swears.

mots.sens: and by what you do not see

versets.trad: this is indeed the word of a noble Messenger,
versets.commentaire: la-qawlu rasūlin karīm (لَقَوْلُ رَسولٍ كَريم): it is the word of a noble Messenger. The answer to the twofold oath: the Quran is a word transmitted by a Messenger honoured with God. Qushayrī: “of high standing with God; the word of the noble Messenger is the Quran, or its recitation.” The Quran is no invention of the Prophet but a word he transmits, he the approved messenger (cf. s. 72, v. 27). The nobility of the bearer guarantees the purity of the message. What will be said in the following verses — neither poet, nor soothsayer — flows from this central affirmation: it is a word received, not fabricated.

mots.racine: ق و ل / ر س ل / ك ر م
mots.sens: it is the word of a noble Messenger
mots.def: qawl: word; rasūl karīm: honoured messenger.

versets.ar: وَمَا هُوَ بِقَوْلِ شَاعِرٍ قَلِيلًا مَّا تُؤْمِنُونَ
versets.tr: wa-mā huwa bi-qawli shāʿirin qalīlan mā tuʾminūna
versets.trad: it is not the word of a poet; how little you believe!
versets.commentaire: wa-mā huwa bi-qawli shāʿir (وَما هُوَ بِقَوْلِ شاعِر): it is not the word of a poet. Qushayrī: “Muhammad is neither poet nor soothsayer.” The Quran exceeds poetry: it has neither the metre nor the vain ornaments of verse, but a truth that transforms. Qalīlan mā tuʾminūn — how little you believe! The reproach pierces: their faith is minimal, that is why they reduce the Word to poetry. To label “poetry” what exceeds poetry is the reflex of one who will not submit. The weakness of faith engenders these false labels.

mots.racine: ق و ل / ش ع ر
mots.sens: it is not the word of a poet

mots.racine: ق ل ل / أ م ن

versets.ar: وَلَا بِقَوْلِ كَاهِنٍ قَلِيلًا مَّا تَذَكَّرُونَ
versets.tr: wa-lā bi-qawli kāhinin qalīlan mā tadhakkarūna
versets.trad: nor is it the word of a soothsayer; how little you reflect!
versets.commentaire: wa-lā bi-qawli kāhin (وَلا بِقَوْلِ كاهِن): nor the word of a soothsayer. After the poet, the soothsayer: two labels by which the Meccans sought to neutralize the Quran. But it is neither. Qalīlan mā tadhakkarūn — how little you remember! To the reproach of lack of faith (v. 41) answers that of lack of reflection: if they truly meditated, they would see that this Word resembles neither the verses of poets nor the oracles of soothsayers. Denial feeds on the refusal to think. To reduce the Quran to a known category excuses one from confronting it in its newness.

mots.racine: ق و ل / ك ه ن
mots.sens: nor the word of a soothsayer

versets.trad: it is a revelation from the Lord of the worlds!
versets.commentaire: tanzīlun min rabbi l-ʿālamīn (تَنْزيلٌ مِنْ رَبِّ العالَمين): a descent from the Lord of the worlds. A central and positive affirmation after the denials: the Quran is neither poetry nor divination, but revelation — a descent (tanzīl) from the Lord of the worlds Himself. Here is its true origin. The whole debate is resolved there: what seems to some madness, poetry, or divination is in reality the Word of God descended upon His messenger. The divine origin explains the power that touches hearts (cf. s. 74, v. 24): it is not the art of a man, but the breath of the Lord.

mots.def: tanzīl: that which is sent down.

mots.racine: ر ب ب / ع ل م
mots.sens: from the Lord of the worlds
mots.def: rabb al-ʿālamīn: Lord of the universes.

versets.trad: Had he invented against Us some false sayings,
versets.commentaire: wa-law taqawwala ʿalaynā (وَلَوْ تَقَوَّلَ عَلَيْنا): and had he forged against Us. A hypothesis to demonstrate the Prophet's faithfulness: if Muhammad invented false sayings ascribing them to God, the chastisement would be immediate (v. 45-46). Qushayrī: “if Muhammad lied about Us, We would prevent him and preserve him from it; and if he did it deliberately, We would chastise him. Belief in the impeccability (ʿiṣma) of the prophets is obligatory.” The reasoning proves the authenticity: that the Prophet is still alive and supported attests that he forged nothing. The faithfulness of the messenger guarantees the truthfulness of the message.

mots.def: taqawwala: to invent words, falsely ascribe.

versets.trad: We would have seized him by the right hand,
versets.commentaire: la-akhadhnā minhu bi-l-yamīn (لَأَخَذْنا مِنْهُ بِاليَمين): We would have seized him by the right hand (by force, or by power). The consequence of the hypothetical imposture: an immediate, firm seizure. The verse underlines, by contrast, the protection the true messenger enjoys: he is not seized, therefore he does not lie. The extreme rigour that would strike the impostor proves, by its absence, the Prophet's authenticity. God does not let one lie in His name: that the mission continues is the sign that it is true.

mots.racine: أ خ ذ / ي م ن
mots.sens: We would have seized him by the right hand
mots.def: akhadha: to seize; yamīn: right hand, force.

versets.trad: then We would have severed his aorta,
versets.commentaire: la-qaṭaʿnā minhu l-watīn (لَقَطَعْنا مِنْهُ الوَتين): We would have severed his aorta. An image of the radical, immediate sanction that would strike whoever lied in God's name. Watīn — the aorta, the vein of life: to sever it is to take life on the spot. The severity of the hypothetical penalty measures the horror of lying about God. And its non-fulfilment is the resounding proof: the Prophet lives, supported — so he did not lie. The argument is unanswerable: if the Word were imposture, its bearer would no longer be there to transmit it.

mots.racine: ق ط ع / و ت ن
mots.sens: We would have severed his aorta

versets.ar: فَمَا مِنكُم مِّنْ أَحَدٍ عَنْهُ حَاجِزِينَ
versets.tr: fa-mā minkum min aḥadin ʿanhu ḥājizīna
versets.trad: and not one of you could have shielded him.
versets.commentaire: fa-mā minkum min aḥadin ʿanhu ḥājizīn (فَما مِنْكُمْ مِنْ أَحَدٍ عَنْهُ حاجِزين): not one of you could have held it back from him. The completion of the hypothesis: were God to decide to chastise the impostor, none could hold Him back. This affirmation of absolute sovereignty closes the demonstration: it is God, and none else, who guards or abandons. That the Prophet is guarded proves that God approves him. None can interpose between God and His decree — neither to save the impostor, nor to strike down the true messenger. The Prophet's protection is the work of God alone, and none can do anything about it.

versets.trad: It is a reminder for the God-fearing,
versets.commentaire: la-tadhkiratun li-l-muttaqīn (لَتَذْكِرَةٌ لِلْمُتَّقين): a reminder for the pious. The Quran is a reminder (tadhkira), but it is received as such only by “those who fear God” (al-muttaqūn). Like the ear that recollects (v. 12), the pious heart makes the reminder its own; the closed heart lets it pass. The same Word falls on all, but only the heart opened by reverent fear recollects it as a treasure. Piety is not the condition of access to the reading, but to true reception: one truly hears the reminder only if one is already turned toward God.

versets.trad: but We know that some of you are deniers!
versets.commentaire: la-naʿlamu anna minkum mukadhdhibīn (لَنَعْلَمُ أَنَّ مِنْكُمْ مُكَذِّبين): We know that among you are deniers. The same reminder that guides the pious (v. 48) is denied by others. And God knows it: nothing of the sorting of hearts escapes Him. The verse recalls that denial is not unknown to God: He knows those who refuse. This knowledge is no idle threat but a lucid statement: before the same Word, hearts part, and that parting is known to the One who probes. That God knows our denials should move us to abandon them.

versets.trad: It is a source of anguish for the disbelievers!
themes: the Quran a source of regret
versets.commentaire: la-ḥasratun ʿalā l-kāfirīn (لَحَسْرَةٌ عَلى الكافِرين): a source of regret for the deniers. The same Quran that is a reminder for the pious (v. 48) will be bitter regret for those who denied it. On the Day, they will see what they refused, and regret (ḥasra) will seize them. One and the same Word: a treasure for whoever receives it, a source of eternal regret for whoever rejects it. The Quran does not change; it is the heart that decides to make it its light or its lament. The future regret of the deniers should be, even now, a warning: there is still time to turn the future regret into present light.

mots.sens: it is a source of regret

versets.trad: It is the Truth of certainty!
versets.commentaire: la-ḥaqqu l-yaqīn (لَحَقُّ اليَقين): the very Truth of certainty. Qushayrī: “ḥaqq al-yaqīn is certainty itself” — the Quran is truth and certainty at the highest degree. He recalls the distinction of the degrees of certainty: ʿilm al-yaqīn (the knowledge of certainty, by proof), ʿayn al-yaqīn (the eye of certainty, by direct vision), ḥaqq al-yaqīn (the reality of certainty, by lived experience, by union). “Men's sciences differ according to the paths that lead to certainty, in obscurity and in clarity.” The Quran is no opinion or conjecture: it is the reality of certainty, what one can no longer doubt once one has tasted it.

mots.racine: ح ق ق / ي ق ن
mots.sens: it is the Truth of certainty
mots.def: ḥaqq al-yaqīn: the very reality of certainty.

versets.trad: So glorify the Name of your Lord, the Magnificent!
themes: the Name of the immense Lord
versets.commentaire: A closing verse: fa-sabbiḥ bi-smi rabbika l-ʿaẓīm (فَسَبِّحْ بِاسْمِ رَبِّكَ العَظيم) — glorify the Name of your Lord the Immense. After the affirmation that the Quran is the Truth of certainty (v. 51), the heart's right response: glorification (tasbīḥ). Before the Immense (al-ʿAẓīm), there is but to acknowledge His grandeur, to declare Him transcendent, above all one ascribes to Him. The sūra that began with the Inevitable and the account of the annihilated peoples ends on this movement of elevation: not dread, but praise. The last word is not chastisement but the glorification of the Name — for to know the Truth leads, in the end, to glorify the One who is the Truth.

mots.racine: س م و / ر ب ب / ع ظ م
mots.sens: the Name of your Lord the Immense
